coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical functionalization of polypyrrole nanowires for the development of ultrasensitive biosensors for detecting microRNA
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jiasheng
Hui, Ni
coredata.dc:description: A simple electrochemical patterning strategy has been developed for the construction of ultrasensitive biosensors based on polyethylene glycol (PEG)-polypyrrole (PPy) nanowires substrate in order to supply improved antifouling performances. PPy nanowires array was produced through electrochemical polymerization of pyrrole at constant potential onto glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface. PPy nanowires surfaces were decorated with 4-armed PEG molecules by electrochemical oxidation of amine groups offered by PEG end chains. The prepared PEG/PPy nanowires have integrated the excellent electrical conductivity of conducting polymer PPy nanowires with the good antifouling property of PEG. MicroRNAs (miRNAs) play very important roles in cancer development and a variety of diseases, which make them become promising biomarkers of the onset, prognosis and risk of diseases. Therefore the establishment of miRNA profiles for diseases and the detection of miRNAs in biological samples are critical milestones in diagnostics. An ultrasensitive electrochemical biosensor for miRNA can be effortlessly developed by the immobilization of DNA probes onto PEG/PPy nanowires. DNA/RNA hybridization was monitored by changes of methylene blue (MB) redox signal using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. A wide linear range (0.10 pM ∼ 1.0 nM) to target miRNA was obtained by the fabricated biosensor, and miRNA mismatches can also be easily identified with satisfactory. Furthermore, the biosensor based on PEG/PPy nanowires can be extended to the development of any type of DNA-based biosensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biosensing
Antifouling property
Polypyrrole nanowires
Polyethylene glycol
MicroRNA
Electrochemical biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of sensing surface fabrication process
Nonspecific protein adsorption tests of PEG/PPy nanowires
EIS characterization of the biosensor construction procedure
Nonfouling, selectivity and reproducibility performances of the biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of PEG/PPy nanowires modified biosensor fabrication process and its antifouling ability. (a) bare GCE (b) PPy/GCE (c) PEG/PPy/GCE (d) C1/PEG/GCE (e) MB/C1/PEG/GCE (f) T2/MB/C1/PEG/GCE (f) DPV results.
SEM images of PPy nanowires on pretreated GCE at different magnifications.
X-ray photoelectron spectra of (A) PPy, (B) PEG/PPy, and (C) C1/PEG/PPy Coated Indium tin oxide (ITO) surface.

Single protein attachment tests of electrodes based on PEG/PPy nanowires (A, C, E) and only PPy nanowires (B, D, F) by EIS technique. EIS images were took records before and after immersing in HSA, HG and LZ solutions (1.0 mg mL−1).
Protein adsorption tests of PPy/GCE (A) and PEG/PPy/GCE (B) using EIS technique. EIS images were recorded before and after immersing in different concentration human serum.

(A) EIS recorded at (a) GCE, (b) PPy/GCE, (c) PEG/PPy/GCE, (d) C1/PEG/PPy/GCE and (e) T2/C1/PEG/PPy/GCE. (B) DPV curve of C1/PEG/PPy/GCE (curve a, black line); DPV characterization of MB/C1/PEG/PPy/GCE recorded before (curve b) and after (curve c) target miRNA (T2) hybridization. The experiment conditions were controlled as followed. (MB concentration: 30 μM; the immersing time in MB: 20 min; the T2 concentration: 1 fM; the hybridization time: 60 min).

(A) Typical DPV spectra corresponding to MB/C1/PEG/PPy/GCE incubated in different target miRNA concentrations: curves From top to bottom represent 0 M, 10−15 M, 10-14 M, 10-13 M, 10-12 M, 10-11 M, 10-10 M, 10-9 M, 10-8 M, 10-7 M, respectively. (B) DPV peak current response changes [Δip/ip0] of the biosensor as a function of microRNA concentrations. The inset exhibits the corresponding calibration curve for the miRNA sensors.

(A) The nonfouling ability of biosensors in serum samples of different concentrations (V/V). (B). Responses of the biosensor (Δip/ip0) to HSA (1.0 μg mL−1), LZ (1.0 μg mL-1), M1 (100 pM), M2 (100 pM), M3 (100 pM) and T2 (1.0 pM), respectively.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical functionalization of polypyrrole nanowires for the development of ultrasensitive biosensors for detecting microRNA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive and low-fouling biosensor for microRNA was developed based on polyethylene glycol (PEG)-polypyrrole (PPy) nanowires substrate. PEG/PPy nanowires have integrated the excellent electrical conductivity of conducting polymer PPy nanowires with the good antifouling property of PEG. The fabricated biosensing interface can respond to microRNA with a strikingly selective and sensitive manner, demonstrated excellent protein-resistant performance even in complex human serum samples.

A simple electrochemical patterning strategy has been developed for the construction of ultrasensitive biosensors based on polyethylene glycol (PEG)-polypyrrole (PPy) nanowires substrate in order to supply improved antifouling performances. PPy nanowires array was produced through electrochemical polymerization of pyrrole at constant potential onto glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface. PPy nanowires surfaces were decorated with 4-armed PEG molecules by electrochemical oxidation of amine groups offered by PEG end chains. The prepared PEG/PPy nanowires have integrated the excellent electrical conductivity of conducting polymer PPy nanowires with the good antifouling property of PEG. MicroRNAs (miRNAs) play very important roles in cancer development and a variety of diseases, which make them become promising biomarkers of the onset, prognosis and risk of diseases. Therefore the establishment of miRNA profiles for diseases and the detection of miRNAs in biological samples are critical milestones in diagnostics. An ultrasensitive electrochemical biosensor for miRNA can be effortlessly developed by the immobilization of DNA probes onto PEG/PPy nanowires. DNA/RNA hybridization was monitored by changes of methylene blue (MB) redox signal using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. A wide linear range (0.10 pM ∼ 1.0 nM) to target miRNA was obtained by the fabricated biosensor, and miRNA mismatches can also be easily identified with satisfactory. Furthermore, the biosensor based on PEG/PPy nanowires can be extended to the development of any type of DNA-based biosensor.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) have attracted more and more attentions because their abnormal expression profiles are closely related to the pathogenesis of many malignant tumors and miRNAs have been recognized as an important class of biomarkers of many diseases, such as human cancers [1–3] and neurological diseases [4,5]. It is of remarkable importance to establish accurate and rapid analytical ways for miRNAs assay. However, there are great challenges in the assay of miRNAs because of their intrinsic characteristics of low abundance, short length and sequence homology [6]. Conventional technologies, such as quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR), microarray and Northern blotting, have intrinsic disadvantages including tedious procedures, low multiplexing capability and high operation cost [7–9]. These disadvantages have limited the application in conventional miRNAs analysis and triggered the development of novel alternative methods. In recent years due to highly attractive perspectives of electrochemical assays including low cost, fast speed and miniaturized devices, a number of electrochemical miRNA biosensing approaches based on nanomaterial or functionalized nanomaterial have been constructed [10–13].

The nanomaterial has been shown to improve the sensitivity of the electrochemical biosensors by increasing the real surface area of the electrodes and accelerating the electron transfer. Polypyrrole (PPy), as a promising functional nanomaterial, has been applied for the construction of bioanalytical sensors due to its environmental stability, facile synthesis, high electric activity, good biocompatibility and versatility [14]. Particularly worth mentioning are PPy nanowires with high alignment, which supply high aspect ratio and short diffusion road lengths to target biomolecules, resulting in high performance [15–17]. For example, Mai et al has reported that probe DNA was immobilized directly to the PPy nanowires via dropping method and the DNA sensors were constructed for the DNA detection using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique [18]. Wang et al have reported that the entrapment approach has been utilized to electropolymerize probe DNA strands into PPy films [19]. However, these methods of biomolecules immobilization suffer from a low reproducibility and an unfavorable spatially controlled deposition. Nanosensors have drawn more and more attention because their appearance can provide a prospect for high sensitivity and rapid assay of analyte [20]. Conducting polymers, such as polyaniline (PANI) and PPy, have been applied to nanosensors [21–24]. For instance, Vinod Kumar Gupta et al. reported that the nanosensor based on PPy film showed a good recognition capacity for tobramycin compared with other structural analogues [25]. Mehmet Lütfi Yola and co-workers have reported that PPy can create homogeneous polymeric film surface and has been used for sensitive determination of cefexime and bilirubin in human plasma [26,27].

The introduction of patterned polyethylene glycol (PEG) onto PPy surface can increase the amount of fixed biomolecules and increase the sensitivity of biosensors [28]. 4-armed PEG terminated with amine groups (PEG-NH2) used in this work possesses a large amount of amine groups, and its molecule is shown in Scheme S1. PEG has been successfully used to construct electrochemical biosensors although PEG is a poor conductor of electricity. For example, Hayat et al. proposed an electrochemical biosensor based on PEG-aptamer two piece macromolecules for the label free detection of ochratoxin A [29]. Kjallman et al developed sensitive electrochemical DNA-based biosensors based on a self-assembled monolayer of PEG and hairpin structured probes [30]. More importantly, PEG series, non-toxic, strongly hydrophilic biocompatible polymers, have been deeply accepted as “gold standard” of anti-biofouling polymers [31–34]. Recently, our group has demonstrated the attachment of PEG polymer onto polyaniline nanowires using covalent linkage. We proved that the introduction of PEG polymer effectively suppressed the nonspecific protein adsorption, and the low fouling and highly sensitive biosensor based on PEG can be applied in complex human serum [35,36].

The literature has reported that non-functionalized PPy surfaces were decorated with amine terminated molecules such as PEG-NH2 and poly(amidoamine) dendrimers (PAMAM) using electrochemical oxidation of their amine groups [28,37,38]. This approach has begun to draw more attention, duo to the merits of this nanocomposite preparation method, such as time-saving and simplicity. For instance, Miodek et al. have established a facile way to fix dendrimers PAMAM of forth generation to PPy modified carbon nanotubes (CNTs) by the electrochemical oxidation of the amine groups of dendrimers PAMAM. Moreover, the amine of PAMAM has been used for further covalent immobilization of biomolecules [37].

In this work, the modification of PPy nanowires with PEG-NH2 was performed by the electro-oxidation of amine groups of PEG, leading to covalent immobilization onto the PPy nanowire surfaces. The resulting PEG/PPy nanowires, integrating the antifouling ability and good biocompatibility of PEG with high electric activity and versatility of PPy nanowires, supply an excellent matrix for the development of an ultrasensitive electrochemical biosensor. Probe DNA was immobilized onto PEG/PPy nanowires for miRNA assays. Methylene Blue (MB) as an excellent redox indicator [39–41] was selected in this work because of its different affinity with ssDNA and dsDNA [6]. As shown in Scheme 1 , Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) signal changes were recorded before and after DNA/RNA hybrid formation. The proposed PEG/PPy nanowires-based biosensor demonstrates enhanced antifouling properties (towards single protein and complex human serum) and good analytical performances (wide linear range, low detection limit, reproducibility and excellent selectivity) for miRNA assays. A limit of detection (LOD) of 0.033 pM was obtained. The proposed electrochemical sensor can be generalized for application with any type of DNA-based biosensor.

4-armed PEG terminated with amine groups (2000 Da) was bought from Suzhou Nord Derivatives Pharm-Tech Co., Ltd. Pyrrole, LiClO4, MB, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimde (NHS) and p-toluenesulfonate acid (TsOH) were purchased from Aladdin Reagents (Shanghai, China). Human serum was acquired from Qingdao Chengyang People's Hospital. Human serum albumin (HSA, PI = 4.8), human hemoglobin (HG, PI = 7.07) lysozyme (LZ, PI = 11.0–11.2) and synthetic oligonucleotides were bought by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The related DNA or miRNA sequences (capture DNA: C1; target DNA: T2; one-base mismatched RNA: M1; three-base mismatched RNA: M2; non-complementary RNA: M3) were shown in Supporting Information (Table S1).
Millipore water was used in all solution preparation, which is obtained from a Milli-Q water purifying system.

JC2000 goniometer was used to perform static water contact angle tests. All electrochemical measurements were finished in CHI660E Electrochemical Workstation (Shanghai CH Instru. Co., China). The modified GCEs (3.0 mm), Ag/AgCl electrode and platinum wire were applied was utilized as the working electrodes, the reference electrode and auxiliary electrode, respectively.

Firstly, GCEs were polished using alumina slurries and electrochemically pretreated in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) according to our previous literature [42]. Secondly, ordered PPy nanowire arrays were constructed through electrochemical polymerization of pyrrole onto the pretreated GCEs in a mixed solution of 0.2 M PBS (pH 6.86), 0.145 M pyrrole and 0.1 M TsOH using a constant potential of 0.75 V for 600 s. PPy/GCE was obtained. Thirdly, PEG-NH2 was grafted onto the PPy nanowires surface through covalent bonding. The process was carried out in a mixed solution including 2.0 mg mL−1 PEG-NH2 and 0.5 M LiClO4 by cyclic voltammetry (the potential: 0.0 ∼1.1 V; 5 cycles; scan rate: 100 mV s−1). The resulting modified electrode was defined as PEG/PPy/GCE, which was rinsed with PBS and Millipore water adequately. Lastly, PEG/PPy/GCEs were subsequently immersed in a solution containing 0.4 M EDC, 0.1 M NHS and 1.0 μM C1 (carboxyl-functionalized single-stranded DNA) solution for 45 min, to allow the covalent immobilization of C1 to the PEG-NH2. The obtained biosensor was denoted as C1/PEG/PPy/GCE.

The C1/PEG/PPy/GCE was immersed in 2 mL of MB (30 μM, 20 min) to integrated MB into the capture DNA (C1), subsequently by rinse with PBS and water respectively. The biosensor was named as MB/C1/PEG/PPy/GCE. The hybridization reaction was then performed by the modified biosensor into PBS (pH 7.4) containing target DNA (T2) for 60 min. The MB electrochemical redox signals of the biosensor (DPV responses) were achieved in PBS (pH 7.4) over potential range of -0.8 to 0.4 V. The biosensor was defined as T2/MB/C1/PEG/PPy/GCE after the hybridization reaction with T2. The DPV parameters are set: potential increment, 4 mV; amplitude, 50 mV; pulse period, 0.5 s. To test the specificity of the prepared biosensor, the mismatch miRNA sequences (M1–M3) were utilized as controls.

To assess the antifouling performances of PEG/PPy nanowires, protein-containing samples were selected, such as single protein solution (1.0 mg mL−1, HSA, LZ and HG) and different concentration human serum samples. EIS technique was utilized to compare nonspecific protein adsorption onto PPy nanowires and PEG/PPy nanowires. EIS tests were completed under the following conditions (current potential: 0.20 V; frequency range: 0.1–100 KHz; the applied sine wave: 5 mV). All EIS tests were performed in PBS (0.2 M, pH 7.4) solution including 0.1 M KCl and 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. Before and after immersing protein-containing samples for 30 min, EIS of modified electrodes were recorded after adequate washing, and the changes were compared.

Ordered PPy nanowires were developed by one-step electrochemical polymerization of pyrrole onto the pretreated GCE surface using TsOH as the “soft” template, at a fixed potential of 0.75 V. Fig. 1 exhibits the SEM images of the as-prepared PPy nanowires array at different magnification. It can be observed that well-regulated PPy nanowires were all arranged vertically on the electrode surface, exhibiting a hierarchical structure by the electroactive conducting polymer nanowires. The larger magnification SEM of PPy nanowire array reveals more detailed morphologies (Fig. 1C–D). PPy nanowires have an average diameter of about 300 nm. The mechanism of aligned nanowire formation has been reported in the previous literatures [14,43,44]. Briefly, Pyrrole monomers are electrically oxidized on the surface of carbon to form oligomers. Then, with the help of “soft” template (TsOH anions), conglomerations of PPy tend to aggregate vertically at the nucleation sites to form one dimensional nanowires. The hierarchical structure of upwardly aligned PPy nanowires supplies a fast electron-transport path and a facile ion diffusion avenue, which is extremely beneficial for constructing a biosensor with high performances.

As shown in Fig. S1, chronocoulometry experiments have demonstrated that the electroactive surface area of the PPy nanowires modified electrode was about 22.61 and 3.34 times larger than that of the bare GCE and the planar PPy-modified electrode, respectively. The results proved that the PPy nanowires modified electrode greatly expanded the active surface area and provided a promising substrate for sensing application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of sensing surface fabrication process
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the hydrophilicity of the prepared nanomaterial, the wetting behavior was estimated through testing the contact angles (Fig. S2 and Table S2). The contact angle of bare ITO is 77.5°, while that of PPy/ITO is 103.5°, indicating far more hydrophobic. As wished, after further attachment of PPy nanowires with PEG, a significant decrease of the contact angle appeared (22.9°), demonstrating extreme hydrophilicity of PEG/PPy nanowires. The contact angle of PEG/PPy nanowires is close to that of other antifouling materials, such as PEG/PANI (22.4°) [35] and the peptide modified surface (19.6°) [45]. More interestingly, the successful attachments of capture probe DNA (C1) onto PEG/PPy nanowires led to a smaller contact angle (15.3°). Previous reports have proved that excellent hydrophilicity of nanomaterials can strongly oppose nonspecific protein adsorption via the formation of a hydration layer [45,46]. Thus, C1/PEG/PPy modified electrode has great potential to use in the complex samples without interference from co-existing proteins.

XPS spectra also confirmed the modifications of the PPy nanowires with PEG and the immobilization of DNA probe. Fig. 2 shows the results of XPS analysis for PPy (A), PEG/PPy (B) and C1/PEG/PPy (C). After functionalization of PPy nanowires with PEG, N 1 s and O 1S peak corresponding to PEG increased apparently, while S 2p peak decreased obviously, which was derived from TsOH doped PPy nanowires. The results confirmed the attachment of PEG onto PPy nanowires. Subsequently, after C1 was fixed to the PEG/PPy nanowires, the N 1 s peak decreased, together with the appearance of a new P 2p peak, proving the successful immobilization of DNA probes. Table S3 showed the quantitative analysis of atomic composition of PPy, PEG/PPy, and C1/PEG/PPy coated ITO surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nonspecific protein adsorption tests of PEG/PPy nanowires
ce:section.ce:para: Nyquist impedance plot, a sensitive electrochemical technique, consists of a semicircle part (the electron transfer limited process under high frequency) and a linear part (the diffusion process under low frequency). The semicircle diameter can be equal to charge transfer resistance (Rct) [47]. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 A, C and E, great changes (large increases of Rct) were observed for PPy/GCE after incubation in HSA, HG or LZ solutions, because a serious nonspecific protein is attached to PPy nanowire that blocks the transfer of charge [48]. While for PEG/PPy/GCE, negligible EIS changes were observed at the same conditions. These results prove that the introduction of PEG onto PPy nanowires can effectively suppress the protein adsorption. To further evaluate the antifouling property of PEG/PPy in real complex biological samples, the modified electrodes were immersed in serum solution of different dilution ratios. Fig. 4 demonstrated EIS plots of PEG/PPy and PPy modified electrodes remembered after sequential immersing in human serum samples. For PEG/PPy/GCE, no observed Rct changes appeared until immersing in 10% human serum sample. While for the control tests of PPy/GCE, notable Rct changes can be seen even after the incubation in 1% serum sample, indicating severe nonspecific protein attachment in real complex samples onto PPy modified electrode. These above results proved that PEG modified PPy nanowires interfaces maintained praiseworthy abilities in suppressing the nonspecific protein attachment even in these natural complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS characterization of the biosensor construction procedure
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the antifouling ability of the PEG/PPy nanowires, a sensitive and nonfouling biosensor for miRNA sequence was constructed. Fig. 5 A illustrated characteristic EIS plots of modified electrodes step by step. Predictably, an Rct decrease of PPy/GCE (12.4 Ω, curves b) was observed compared with that of bare GCE (121.4 Ω, curves a), which benefited from the fact that PPy nanowires demonstrate good electronic conductivity [49]. The Rct grew after attachment of PEG to PPy nanowires (498.0 Ω, curves c), because PEG is non-conductive and can prevent the electron transfer to a certain extent. It is worth mentioning that the value is far smaller than previously reported only PEG modification interfaces (∼120 kΩ) [47], demonstrating that PEG/PPy nanowires are good conductive. Then, the Rct of C1/PEG/PPy/GCE went up after probe DNA (C1) immobilization (619.3 Ω, curve d), because both the phosphate skeletons of DNA and [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probes are negatively charged and they are mainly repelled to each other [50]. An obvious increase in Rct (938.3 Ω, curve e) can be seen after C1 hybridized with target miRNA (T2), because of the formation of DNA/RNA hybrids. Thus, an electrochemical biosensor can be constructed successfully.

An electrochemical indicator (MB) was utilized to detect miRNA through DPV signal change after the hybridization reaction between attached capture DNA (C1) and target miRNA (T2). The ipeak of MB/C1/PEG/PPy/GCE is regarded as background signal (i0) and the peak current change rate of MB signal (Δip/ip0) after DNA/RNA hybridization has a corresponding relationship with the miRNA concentration. Fig. 5B shows the characteristic DPV responses of the MB/C1/PEG/PPy/GCE before and after (curve b and c) hybridization with miRNA. The MB/C1/PEG/PPy/GCE and T2/MB/C1/PEG/PPy/GCE exhibit well-known DPV responses at about −0.19 V, which are produced by the electrochemical oxidation of MB. The oxidation ipeak (peak current of DPV) of MB after miRNA hybridization lessens obviously compared with that of MB/C1/PEG/PPy/GCE. This is because MB shows a much higher binding force to ssDNA capture probe rather than a dsDNA (or DNA/RNA) hybrid, which has been proved by spectroscopy study [39,51]. So, with hybridization reaction of target miRNA (T2) to the attached probe DNA (C1), a series of consecutive changes in peak current of DPV were observed (Fig. 6 ). At the same time this current variation rate (Δip/ip0) can be utilized as signal for detection of target miRNA.

In order to improve sensitivity and efficiency of prepared biosensors, the optimization for the concentration of MB, MB adsorption time onto probe DNA (C1), immobilization time for probe DNA (C1), and miRNA (T2) hybridization reaction time on the biosensor were discussed item by item. As shown in Fig. S3 A–D, after carefully survey, the optimum conditions were as follow: (A) The concentration of MB, 30 μM; (B) MB interaction time, 20 min; (C) immobilization time for probe DNA, 45 min; and (D) miRNA hybridization time on the biosensor, 60 min.

The sensing properties of prepared biosensors for the assay of miRNA were studied in PBS using DPV by measuring the MB redox signal. The current response of the biosensor lessened in accordance with the increased concentration of miRNA (Fig. 6A), because of the hybridization reaction of miRNA selectively and the formation of DNA/RNA hybrids. More detailed analyses (Fig. 6B) exhibited that the DPV peak current changes [Δip/ip0] showed a linear correlation with the logarithmic values of miRNA concentrations across 0.1 pM to 1.0 nM (5 order dynamic ranges). Δip/ip0 = 1.218 + 0.0338 log C (R2 = 0.995) was obtained with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.033 pM (S/N = 3). The analytical performance of the proposed biosensor was superior to previous biosensors (Table S4). Obviously, the biosensor obtained a far lower detection limit [52–56]. Excellent analytical performances of the biosensor are mainly due to the biocompatibility and antifouling ability of PEG/PPy nanowires and the specific strong binding force between the capture DNA and the target miRNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nonfouling, selectivity and reproducibility performances of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The biosensor antifouling capability was further evaluate by recording the signal changes in the human serum. Note that in order to remove the possible influence of the original miRNA in human serum, the prepared biosensor is saturated with the target miRNA before immersing serum samples. Obviously, the proposed biosensor showed small current change of 0.5% after immersing in 2% serum sample (Fig. 7 A). Even immersing in 10% serum sample, biosensors still kept 85.5% of the original value, demonstrating superior antifouling property of biosensors in natural biological media.

Selectivity is a key criterion to measure successful biosensor performance. The response Δip/ip0 (%) of the biosensor was assessed in HSA, LZ, M1, M2, M3 or T2 solution respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 7B, biosensors showed outstanding selectivity towards target miRNA, and responded much less to mismatched miRNA or proteins (HSA or LZ) even under much higher concentration.

For the biosensor reproducibility experiments, six developed biosensors independently were utilized for target miRNA detection (100 pM), and the relative standard deviation (RSD 5.95%) was obtained. As-prepared biosensors were utilized for assays of the same concentration target miRNA for six times at the same conditions, and RSD was 3.05%. All results indicated excellent reproducibility of electrochemical nonfouling biosensors.

The application prospect of as-prepared biosensors was investigated. Prior to assays, target probe T2 of different concentration was specifically added into 5% (V/V) of practical serum samples. The analytical results were shown in Table S5, with recoveries distribution from 98.2% to 103.7% and the RSD from 3.54% to 4.91%, demonstrating capability of practical application of PEG/PPy nanowires-based biosensor in complex real samples.

In summary, sensitive, selective and nonfouling electrochemical biosensors were constructed, based on DPV technique and PEG/PPy nanowires. PEG/PPy nanowires can be acquired by electrochemical oxidation of amine groups of PEG, leading to covalent immobilization onto the PPy nanowires surfaces. The crucial merit of PEG attachment through electro-oxidation is proved by the simplicity and easy operability, with no need of any pre-chemical preprocessing. Additionally, we demonstrate that antifouling properties of PEG/PPy nanowires can be greatly heightened with no loss of the biosensor sensitivity. Besides its antifouling ability, the immobilized PEG also used as matrix for DNA probe attachment. The developed biosensor demonstrated good selectivity, and can also distinguish miRNA mismatches with satisfactory selectivity. This strategy for biosensors fabrication can be applied to detect a wide range of biomarkers in complex media.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21705088), the Shandong Science and Technology Program (J14LB14), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2016BM05, ZR2017MEE016 and ZR2017BD038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.131.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Circulating microRNA in body fluid: a new potential biomarker for cancer diagnosis and prognosis
Oncomirs – microRNAs with a role in cancer
MicroRNAs in neurodegenerative diseases and their therapeutic potential
MicroRNA control in the immune system: basic principles
Carbon nanotube-polyamidoamine dendrimer hybrid-modified electrodes for highly sensitive electrochemical detection of microRNA24
Real-time quantification of microRNAs by stem-loop RT-PCR
Reliability and reproducibility issues in DNA microarray measurements
MicroRNA detection by northern blotting using locked nucleic acid probes
Multiplexed and amplified electronic sensor for the detection of microRNAs from cancer cells
Direct quantification of MicroRNA at low picomolar level in sera of glioma patients using a competitive hybridization followed by amplified voltammetric detection
Four-way junction formation promoting ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of MicroRNA
Trace and label-free microRNA detection using oligonucleotide encapsulated silver nanoclusters as probes
Conducting polymer nanowire arrays with enhanced electrochemical performance
High rate capabilities Fe3O4-based Cu nano-architectured electrodes for lithium-ion battery applications
Fast electrochemistry of conductive polymer nanotubes: synthesis, mechanism, and application
Effective immobilization of DNA for development of polypyrrole nanowires based biosensor
New label-free DNA recognition based on doping nucleic-acid probes within conducting polymer films
Sensor array techniques for mimicking the mammalian olfactory system
Sensitive determination of citrinin based on molecular imprinted electrochemical sensor
Sensitive analysis of simazine based on platinum nanoparticles on polyoxometalate/multi-walled carbon nanotubes
A highly efficient nanomaterial with molecular imprinting polymer: carbon nitride nanotubes decorated with graphene quantum dots for sensitive electrochemical determination of chlorpyrifos
Molecular imprinting polymer with polyoxometalate/carbon nitride nanotubes for electrochemical recognition of bilirubin
Molecular imprinted polypyrrole modified glassy carbon electrode for the determination of tobramycin

Molecularly imprinted electrochemical biosensor based on Fe@ Au nanoparticles involved in 2-aminoethanethiol functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes for sensitive determination of cefexime in human plasma
Palladium nanoparticles functionalized graphene quantum dots with molecularly imprinted polymer for electrochemical analysis of citrinin
Electro-engineered polymeric films for the development of sensitive aptasensors for prostate cancer marker detection
Design of PEG-aptamer two piece macromolecules as convenient and integrated sensing platform: application to the label free detection of small size molecules
Effect of probe density and hybridization temperature on the response of an electrochemical hairpin-DNA sensor
Interaction of blood plasma with antifouling surfaces
Efficient antifouling surface for quantitative surface plasmon resonance based biosensor analysis
Basic physicochemical properties of polyethylene glycol coated gold nanoparticles that determine their interaction with cells
Poly(ethylene glycol) in drug delivery: dros and cons as well as potential alternatives
PEGylated polyaniline nanofibers: antifouling and conducting biomaterial for electrochemical DNA sensing
Gold nanoparticles and polyethylene glycols functionalized conducting polyaniline nanowires for ultrasensitive and low fouling immunosensing of alpha-fetoprotein
E-DNA sensor of Mycobacterium tuberculosis based on electrochemical assembly of nanomaterials (MWCNTs/PPy/PAMAM)
Electrochemical functionalization of polypyrrole through amine oxidation of poly(amidoamine) dendrimers: application to DNA biosensor
Methylene blue as a novel electrochemical hybridization Indicator
Mutation detection by electro catalysis at DNA-modified electrodes
Electrochemical biosensor for detection of BCR/ABL fusion gene using locked nucleic acids on 4-aminobenzenesulfonic acid-modified glassy carbon electrode
Nanocomposite and nanoporous polyaniline conducting polymers exhibit enhanced catalysis of nitrite reduction
Vertically oriented polypyrrole nanowire arrays on Pd-plated Nafion® membrane and its application in direct methanol fuel cells
Ordered polypyrrole nanowire arrays grown on a carbon cloth substrate for a high-performance pseudocapacitor electrode
Improved biocompatibility and antifouling property of polypropylene non-woven fabric membrane by surface grafting zwitterionic polymer
Zwitterionische Polymere mit antimikrobiellen und Nonfouling‐Eigenschaften
The label free picomolar detection of insulin in blood serum
Polypyrrole functionalized with new copper complex as platform for His-tag antibody immobilization and direct antigen detection
Kinetics of the degradation of electrical conductivity in polypyrrole
Graphene sheets, polyaniline and AuNPs based DNA sensor for electrochemical determination of BCR/ABL fusion gene with functional hairpin probe
A novel surface plasmon resonance biosensor for enzyme-free and highly sensitive detection of microRNA based on multi component nucleic acid enzyme (MNAzyme)-mediated catalyzed hairpin assembly
Gold nano particle decorated graphene core first generation PAMAM dendrimer for label free electrochemical DNA hybridization sensing
DNA electrochemical biosensor based on thionine-graphene nanocomposite
Label-free electrochemical DNA biosensor for rapid detection of mutidrug resistance gene based on Au nanoparticles/toluidine blue–graphene oxide nanocomposites
MicroRNA-mediated signal amplification coupled with GNP/dendrimers on a mass-sensitive biosensor and its applications in intracellular microRNA quantification

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Jiasheng Wang received his PhD degree from Nanjing Agricultural University in 2009. He is currently working for Qingdao Agricultural University as an Associate Professor. His scientific interest is focused on material engineering.

Prof. Ni Hui received her PhD degree from Qingdao University of Science and Technology in 2017. She is working for Qingdao Agricultural University as a Professor. Her scientific interests are focused on electrochemistry and conducting polymers, and she has published more than 20 papers in peer-reviewed journals.
